is a style of music that originated in [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") from [Afro-Trinidadians](/wiki/Afro%E2%80%93Trinidadians_and_Tobagonians "Afro–Trinidadians and Tobagonians") during the early- to mid-19th century and spread to the rest of the Caribbean [Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles") by the mid-20th century. Its rhythms can be traced back to West African [Kaiso](/wiki/Kaiso "Kaiso") and the arrival of French [planters](/wiki/Planter_class "Planter class") and their [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves") from the [French Antilles](/wiki/French_Antilles "French Antilles") in the 18th century.

**Calypso** is a style of music that originated in [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") from [Afro-Trinidadians](/wiki/Afro%E2%80%93Trinidadians_and_Tobagonians "Afro–Trinidadians and Tobagonians") during the early- to mid-19th century and spread to the rest of the Caribbean [Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles") by the mid-20th century. Its rhythms can be traced back to West African [Kaiso](/wiki/Kaiso "Kaiso") and the arrival of French [planters](/wiki/Planter_class "Planter class") and their [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves") from the [French Antilles](/wiki/French_Antilles "French Antilles") in the 18th century.

It is characterized by highly rhythmic and harmonic vocals, and was historically most often sung in a [French creole](/wiki/French-based_creole_languages "French-based creole languages") and led by a [griot](/wiki/Griot "Griot"). As calypso developed, the role of the griot became known as a *[chantuelle](/wiki/Cariso "Cariso")* and eventually, *[calypsonian](/wiki/Calypsonian "Calypsonian")*. As English replaced "patois" ([Antillean](/wiki/Antillean_Creole "Antillean Creole")) as the dominant language, calypso migrated into English, and in so doing it attracted more attention from the government. It allowed the masses to challenge the actions of the unelected Governor and Legislative Council, and the elected town councils of [Port of Spain](/wiki/Port_of_Spain "Port of Spain") and [San Fernando](/wiki/San_Fernando,_Trinidad_and_Tobago "San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago"). Calypso continued to play an important role in political expression.

Calypso in the Caribbean includes a range of genres, including [benna](/wiki/Benna_(genre) "Benna (genre)") in [Antigua and Barbuda](/wiki/Music_of_Antigua_and_Barbuda "Music of Antigua and Barbuda"); [mento](/wiki/Mento "Mento"), a style of [Jamaican](/wiki/Music_of_Jamaica "Music of Jamaica") [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") that greatly influenced [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), the precursor to [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), and reggae; [spouge](/wiki/Spouge "Spouge"), a style of Barbadian popular music; [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica") [cadence-lypso](/wiki/Cadence-lypso "Cadence-lypso"), which mixed calypso with the [cadence](/wiki/Cadence_rampa "Cadence rampa") of [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"); and [soca music](/wiki/Soca_music "Soca music"), a style of [kaiso](/wiki/Kaiso "Kaiso")/calypso, with influences from [chutney](/wiki/Chutney_music "Chutney music"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), Latin and cadence-lypso.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calypso_music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

It is thought that the name "calypso" was originally "kaiso" which is now believed to come from [Efik](/wiki/Efik_language "Efik language") "ka isu" ("go on!") and [Ibibio](/wiki/Ibibio_language "Ibibio language") "kaa iso" ("continue, go on"), used in urging someone on or in backing a contestant.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) There is also a Trinidadian term "cariso" that means "old-time" calypsos.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The term "calypso" is recorded from the 1930s onwards. Alternatively, the insert for *The Rough Guide to Calypso and Soca* (published by [World Music Network](/wiki/World_Music_Network "World Music Network")) favours John Cowley's arguments in *Carnival, Canboulay and Calypso: Traditions in the Making,* that the word might be a corruption of the French *carrouseaux* and through the process of patois and Anglicization became *caliso* and then finally "calypso"; however, Cowley also notes that the first mention of the word "calypso" is given in a description of a dance in 1882 by Abbé Masse.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## History of calypso

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calypso_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History of calypso")]

Calypso music has its roots in [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad "Trinidad") in the 17th century from the West African [Kaiso](/wiki/Kaiso "Kaiso") and [Canboulay](/wiki/Canboulay "Canboulay") familiar to the enslaved Africans brought to the Caribbean islands to work on sugar plantations.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The Africans brought to toil on sugar plantations were stripped of all connections to their homeland and family and their communication opportunities were limited. They used calypso to mock the slave masters and to communicate with each other. Many early calypsos were sung in [French Creole](/wiki/French-based_creole_languages "French-based creole languages") by an individual called a [griot](/wiki/Griot "Griot"). As calypso developed, the role of the griot became known as a *chantuelle* and eventually, *[calypsonian](/wiki/Calypsonian "Calypsonian")*.

Modern calypso, however, began in the 19th century as a fusion of disparate elements ranging from the [masquerade](/wiki/Masquerade_ceremony "Masquerade ceremony") song *[lavway](/wiki/Lavway "Lavway")*, French Creole *[belair](/wiki/B%C3%A8l%C3%A8_(dance) "Bèlè (dance)")* and the [calinda](/wiki/Calinda "Calinda") stick-fighting [chantwell](/wiki/Chantwell "Chantwell"). Also early in its recording years were influences from Venezuelan [paseos](/wiki/Pasillo "Pasillo").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Calypso's early rise was closely connected with the adoption of Carnival by [Trinidadian](/wiki/Trinidadian "Trinidadian") slaves, including canboulay drumming and the music masquerade processions. The French brought [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") to Trinidad, and calypso competitions at Carnival grew in popularity, especially after the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") in 1834.

## Recordings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calypso_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recordings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Trinidadian_Calypso_group_Lovey%27s_String_Band.jpg/250px-Trinidadian_Calypso_group_Lovey%27s_String_Band.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trinidadian_Calypso_group_Lovey%27s_String_Band.jpg)

Lovey's String Band (pre-1925)

The first identifiably calypso genre song was recorded in 1912, by [Lovey's String Band](/wiki/Lovey%27s_String_Band "Lovey's String Band") while visiting [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). In 1914, the second calypso recordings—including the first sung in English—were done by [chantwell](/wiki/Chantwell "Chantwell") [Julian Whiterose](/wiki/Julian_Whiterose "Julian Whiterose"), better known as the Iron Duke and famous calinda stick-fighter. Jules Sims would also record vocal calypsos. The majority of these calypsos of the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") era were instrumentals by Lovey and [Lionel Belasco](/wiki/Lionel_Belasco "Lionel Belasco").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Perhaps due to the constraints of the wartime economy, no recordings of note were produced until the late 1920s and early 1930s, when the "golden era" of calypso would cement the style, form, and phrasing of the music.

Calypso evolved into a way of spreading news around Trinidad. Politicians, [journalists](/wiki/Journalists "Journalists") and public figures often debated the content of each song, and many islanders considered these songs the most reliable news source. [Calypsonians](/wiki/Calypsonian "Calypsonian") pushed the boundaries of free speech as their lyrics spread news of any topic relevant to island life, including speaking out against political corruption.

Even with this censorship, calypsos continued to push boundaries, with a variety of ways to slip songs past the scrutinizing eyes of the editor. [Double entendre](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre"), or double-speak, was one way, as was the practice of denouncing countries such as [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and its [annexation of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"), while making pointed references toward the colonial government's policies in Trinidad. Sex, scandal, gossip, politics, local news, and insulting other calypsonians were the order of the day in classic calypso, just as it is today with classic [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop"). And just as the hip-hop of today, the music sparked shock and outrage in moralistic sections of society.

Countless recordings were dumped at sea in the name of censorship, although in truth, rival US companies did this in the spirit of underhanded competition, claiming that the rivals' material was unfit for US consumption. [Decca Records](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") lost untold pressings in this manner, as did its rival, [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation")'s Bluebird label.

An entrepreneur named Eduardo de Sá Gomes played a significant role in spreading calypso in its early days. Sá Gomes, a Portuguese immigrant who owned a local music and [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") equipment shop in [Port of Spain](/wiki/Port_of_Spain "Port of Spain"), promoted the genre and gave financial support to the local artists. In March 1934, he sent [Roaring Lion](/wiki/Roaring_Lion_(calypsonian) "Roaring Lion (calypsonian)") and [Attila the Hun](/wiki/Attila_the_Hun_(calypsonian) "Attila the Hun (calypsonian)") to New York City to record; they became the first calypsonians to record abroad, bringing the genre out of the West Indies and into pop culture.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) [Lord Invader](/wiki/Lord_Invader "Lord Invader") was quick to follow, and stayed in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") after a protracted legal case involving the theft of his song "[Rum and Coca-Cola](/wiki/Rum_and_Coca-Cola "Rum and Coca-Cola")", a hit by the [Andrews Sisters](/wiki/Andrews_Sisters "Andrews Sisters"). He made his home there along with [Wilmoth Houdini](/wiki/Wilmoth_Houdini "Wilmoth Houdini"), and became one of the great calypsonians of the US.

Early forms of calypso were also similar to [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") (which came after) such as Sans Humanitae. In this [extempo](/wiki/Extempo "Extempo") ([extemporaneous](/wiki/Extemporaneous "Extemporaneous")) melody calypsonians lyricise impromptu, commenting socially or insulting each other, "sans humanité" or "no mercy" (which is again a reference to French influence).

## Popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calypso_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Popularity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Calypso22.jpg/250px-Calypso22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calypso22.jpg)

[Lord Invader](/wiki/Lord_Invader "Lord Invader"), [Mighty Growler](/wiki/Mighty_Growler "Mighty Growler"), [Atilla the Hun (calypsonian)](/wiki/Atilla_the_Hun_(calypsonian) "Atilla the Hun (calypsonian)") and [Roaring Lion](/wiki/Roaring_Lion_(calypsonian) "Roaring Lion (calypsonian)") (1943)

The first major stars of calypso started crossing over to new audiences worldwide in the late 1930s. Attila the Hun, Roaring Lion and [Lord Invader](/wiki/Lord_Invader "Lord Invader") were first, followed by [Lord Kitchener](/wiki/Lord_Kitchener_(calypsonian) "Lord Kitchener (calypsonian)"), one of the longest-lasting calypso stars in history—he continued to release hit records until his death in 2000. 1944's "[Rum and Coca-Cola](/wiki/Rum_and_Coca-Cola "Rum and Coca-Cola")" by the [Andrews Sisters](/wiki/Andrews_Sisters "Andrews Sisters"), a [cover version](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") of a Lord Invader song, became an American hit despite the song being a very critical commentary on the explosion of prostitution, inflation and other negative influences accompanying the American military bases in Trinidad at the time.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Perhaps the most straightforward way to describe the focus of calypso is that it articulated itself as a form of protest against the authoritarian colonial culture which existed at the time.

| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) You may hear arrangements of Calypso music by [Leonard De Paur](/wiki/Leonard_De_Paur "Leonard De Paur") on the album *Calypso Christmas* performed with the De Paur Chorus in 1956 [**Here on archive.org**](https://archive.org/details/CALYPSOCHIRSTMAS/02.DeVirginMaryHadABabyBoy.mp3) | |

In 1956 Mighty Sparrow won Trinida's Music contest. Calypso, especially a toned-down, commercial variant, became a worldwide craze with pop song "[Banana Boat Song](/wiki/Banana_Boat_Song "Banana Boat Song")", or "Day-O", a traditional [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") folk song, was recorded by pop singer [Harry Belafonte](/wiki/Harry_Belafonte "Harry Belafonte") on his album *[Calypso](/wiki/Calypso_(album) "Calypso (album)")* (1956). *Calypso* was the first calypso record to sell more than a million copies. 1956 also saw the massive international hit "[Jean and Dinah](/wiki/Jean_and_Dinah "Jean and Dinah")" by [Mighty Sparrow](/wiki/Mighty_Sparrow "Mighty Sparrow").[[9]](#cite_note-9) This song too was a sly commentary as a "plan of action" for the calypsonian on the widespread prostitution and the prostitutes' desperation after the closing of the U.S. [naval base](/wiki/Naval_base "Naval base") on Trinidad at [Chaguaramas](/wiki/Chaguaramas,_Trinidad "Chaguaramas, Trinidad"). In addition, the choral director [Leonard De Paur](/wiki/Leonard_De_Paur "Leonard De Paur") recorded a calypso album in 1956 for [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") featuring his choral arrangements of traditional Christmas music from Trinidad and Barbados, as well as the song [Mary's Little Boy Child](/wiki/Mary%27s_Boy_Child "Mary's Boy Child") by [Jester Hairston](/wiki/Jester_Hairston "Jester Hairston") (*Calypso Christmas*, CL 923 Mono LP, 1956).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the [Broadway-theatre](/wiki/Broadway_theater "Broadway theater") [musical](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre") *[Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_(musical) "Jamaica (musical)")* (1957), [Harold Arlen](/wiki/Harold_Arlen "Harold Arlen") and [Yip Harburg](/wiki/Yip_Harburg "Yip Harburg") cleverly [parodied](/wiki/Parody_music "Parody music") "commercial" Belafonte-style calypso. Several films jumped on the calypso craze in 1957 such as *[Island in the Sun](/wiki/Island_in_the_Sun_(film) "Island in the Sun (film)")* ([20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox")) that featured Belafonte and the low-budget films *[Calypso Joe](/wiki/Calypso_Joe "Calypso Joe")* ([Allied Artists](/wiki/Allied_Artists_Pictures_Corporation "Allied Artists Pictures Corporation")), *[Calypso Heat Wave](/wiki/Calypso_Heat_Wave "Calypso Heat Wave")* ([Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures")), and *[Bop Girl Goes Calypso](/wiki/Bop_Girl_Goes_Calypso "Bop Girl Goes Calypso")* ([United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists")).
[Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum") released an album, *Calypso...Is Like So* (1957), on [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"), capturing the sound, spirit, and subtleties of the genre.
[Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie") recorded a calypso album *Jambo Caribe* (1964) with [James Moody](/wiki/James_Moody_(saxophonist) "James Moody (saxophonist)") and [Kenny Barron](/wiki/Kenny_Barron "Kenny Barron").

Soul shouter [Gary "US" Bonds](/wiki/Gary_U.S._Bonds "Gary U.S. Bonds") released a calypso album *Twist up Calypso* (1962) on Legrand records, shortly after returning home from his military post in Port of Spain. Nithi Kanagaratnam from Sri Lanka sang calypso-styled songs in Tamil in 1968, which was a success and earned him the title "Father of Tamil Popular Music". Since Baila rhythm was popular in Sri Lanka, most of his songs were classified as Tamil Baila.

In the mid-1970s, women entered the calypso men's-oriented arena. [Calypso Rose](/wiki/Calypso_Rose "Calypso Rose") was the first woman to win the Trinidad Road March competition in 1977 with her song "Gimme More Tempo". The following year with "Come Leh We Jam", she won the "Calypso King " competition, the first time a woman had received the award. The competition's title was changed to Calypso Monarch in her honor.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The French and pioneer electronic musician [Jean Michel Jarre](/wiki/Jean_Michel_Jarre "Jean Michel Jarre") released an album in 1990 called *[Waiting for Cousteau](/wiki/Waiting_for_Cousteau "Waiting for Cousteau")*. The album has four tracks: "Calypso", "Calypso part 2", "Calypso part 3 Fin de Siècle" and "Waiting for Cousteau". It was dedicated to [Jacques-Yves Cousteau](/wiki/Jacques-Yves_Cousteau "Jacques-Yves Cousteau") on his 80th birthday. This album had a special participation of the Amocco Renegades (a traditional steel-drum band from [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago")). In the first track is possible to notice a strong style influence. Calypso had another short burst of commercial interest when [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton")'s horror/comedy film *[Beetlejuice](/wiki/Beetlejuice "Beetlejuice")* (1988) was released, and used Belafonte's "[Jump in the Line](/wiki/Jump_in_the_Line_(Shake,_Senora) "Jump in the Line (Shake, Senora)")" as the soundtrack's headliner and also "The Banana Boat Song" in the dinner-party scene. [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney")'s song "[Under the Sea](/wiki/Under_the_Sea "Under the Sea")", a calypso theme from *[The Little Mermaid](/wiki/The_Little_Mermaid_(1989_film) "The Little Mermaid (1989 film)")*, won an [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song") in 1989 as well as the [Grammy Award for Best Song Written for Visual Media](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Song_Written_for_Visual_Media "Grammy Award for Best Song Written for Visual Media") in 1991.

In the late-1970s a new style, dubbed soca music, emerged from the blending of calypso with elements of East Indian music as well as African American soul, funk, and rock. The soca music of the 1980s featured fast tempos, electric guitars and synthesizers, prominent melodic bass lines, and lyrics celebrating sensuality and dance. Many influential calypso singers including [Ras Shorty I](/wiki/Ras_Shorty_I "Ras Shorty I"), [Mighty Sparrow](/wiki/Mighty_Sparrow "Mighty Sparrow"), [Lord Kitchener](/wiki/Lord_Kitchener_(calypsonian) "Lord Kitchener (calypsonian)"), [Calypso Rose](/wiki/Calypso_Rose "Calypso Rose"), Super Blue, and [David Rudder](/wiki/David_Rudder "David Rudder") embraced the new style.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Brooklyn became an important site for the production of soca records from the late 1970s through the early 1990s.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Although calypso's native land is Trinidad, it is also very popular in [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"). Dominicans, similar to Trinidadians also developed a keen interest in Caribbean genres such as Soca music, and Calypso in the late 1960s.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Called *Kaiso* in French creole, Calypso is illustrated as a unique form of music, especially during the Carnival season. Dominicans mainly use this genre to express their concerns and feedback on the everyday affairs and happenings of their country. Most of the music pieces composed normally have a negative stigma attached to them, expressing dissatisfaction with how their current government choose to conduct the affairs of the country. The first Calypso monarch in Dominica who was crowned in 1959 was called "The Observer" and the longest reigning Calypso monarch is King Dice. The Calypso tent is not just limited to adult participation. There is also a section called Junior monarch[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) where young children under the age of 14 are able to prepare and compete with their personally made Calypso pieces.

Calypso competitions usually commence in January and culminate in the Calypso monarch competition that are usually held the Saturday before Carnival in February. The winner becomes the Calypso King of that year and joins Miss Dominica, the carnival pageant winner of that year, in the traditional costume parade on Carnival Monday.

## Sociopolitical influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calypso_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sociopolitical influence")]

Calypso music has been used by [Calypsonians](/wiki/Calypsonian "Calypsonian") to provide sociopolitical commentary. Prior to the independence of [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), calypsonians would use their music to express the daily struggles of living in Trinidad, critique racial and economic inequalities, express opinions on social order, and voice overall concerns for those living on the island.[[17]](#cite_note-Blatter-17) During the colonial era, the Black lower class used calypso music to protest their poor economic situation and the discrimination which they were subjected to. Calypso music frequently was used as a form of musical protest.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

During the independence movements of Trinidad and Tobago through the early 1950s up until 1962 when the nation gained independence, calypso lyrics frequently critiqued [British colonial rule](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). Lyrics were made to express feelings towards colonial rule as being immoral and oppressive to Caribbean people. In particular, during the movement to independence, calypso music would include common messages of a desire for independence, [opposition to colonial rule](/wiki/Anti-imperialism "Anti-imperialism") and empowerment for people of African descent.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

[Neville Marcano](/wiki/Neville_Marcano "Neville Marcano"), known as the [Growling Tiger](/wiki/Growling_Tiger "Growling Tiger"), became notorious for creating songs calling for independence of Trinidad and Tobago. In his song titled "Abraham Lincoln Speech at Gettysburg", Tiger used inspirations from [Abraham Lincoln's](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") famous [Gettysburg address](/wiki/Gettysburg_Address "Gettysburg Address") to draw on values of liberty, equality and democracy.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) These three principles paralleled some of many ideas circulating during the nationalist movement in [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"). His lyrics struck with those in support of an independent Trinidadian nation hoping to instill similar principles in their own free nation.

[Lord Kitchener](/wiki/Lord_Kitchener_(calypsonian) "Lord Kitchener (calypsonian)"), a largely known Calypsonian, became noted for his politically critical lyrics in his music. Kitchener used calypso to shed light on the grievances of the [windrush generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) a generation of Caribbean families migrating from the islands to England in response to increased labor demands after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Kitchener's 1948 song "Windrush" was written in two versions. The first version gained more global popularity as the lyrics expressed gratitude and appreciation for British colonial rule. However, second version found greater popularity amongst Caribbean people themselves as the lyrics conveyed a story of West Indian immigrants facing discrimination and cultural alienation while living in Britain.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Although Kitchener's alternate version of "Windrush" did not gain as much commercial popularity, the duality of the two versions exemplify how calypso music was used as an outlet for social commentary.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After Trinidad and Tobago gained independence in 1962, calypso music continued to be used as an outlet for political commentary. With [Eric Williams](/wiki/Eric_Williams "Eric Williams") serving as the first Prime Minister of an independent Trinidad and Tobago, calypsonian [Mighty Sparrow](/wiki/Mighty_Sparrow "Mighty Sparrow") released his song "William the Conqueror" where he praises Williams' victory and prides the island in its newfound independence.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Sparrow sings:

> I am no politician, but I could understand if it wasn't for Brother Willie and his ability, Trinidad wouldn't go neither come. We used to vote for food and rum but nowadays we eating all the Indians and them. And in the ending, we voting PNM. Praise little Eric, rejoice and be glad. We have a better future here in Trinidad.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

Calypso music has also been used by politicians to promote political agendas through Calypso competitions. The origins of calypso competitions dates back to the early 19th century post [emancipation](/wiki/Emancipation "Emancipation") where formerly enslaved communities would hold calypso wars showcasing their singing and dancing.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) Later in 1953 Calypso competitions held the same showcasing nature, but became politicized as the [People's National Movement](/wiki/People%27s_National_Movement "People's National Movement") (PNM) took over as the main organizer of competitions.[[17]](#cite_note-Blatter-17) The PNM used the competitions to combat social and class divisions by attracting participants of varying social status to participate and attend the competitions. The idea was to claim a national cultural identity and promote national unity.

* Calypsos have also closely described the evolution of West Indian cricket with more than 500 [calypsos about cricket](/wiki/List_of_calypso_songs_about_cricket "List of calypso songs about cricket").
* A number of calypsos describe wars or the effects of war, see [List of calypso songs about war](/wiki/List_of_calypso_songs_about_war "List of calypso songs about war").
